speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, John. Say, this is tape three.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape number three with Mr. Isadore Horne, January 12, 1996. OK, you and Benjamin got up that morning when it was light and you were starting to look around.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to see where the grave is, just we wanted to see. And tragically, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we just couldn't stay there too long because we were just crying. The ground was still shakin'. Maybe there were still a lot of people maybe were not dead yet. I don't know. We stayed there just for five minutes, and we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you hear anything from the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we didn't hear anything. And we left. We left. Then we decided, what are we going to do now? So we decided we're going to go and just live in the woods. The woods are big and large, deep for hundreds and hundreds of kilometer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went over there, and we find a place over there. Was not too far from-- some Polish man lived over there, not too far. So we figured, well, I don't know, maybe we'll try to make some contact with him. Not all the people are bad. Not all are murderers. And we walked in to him, and we told him. He was a Polish man. He lived by himself. And we says, we just survived from-- he says, I heard what happened, he says. You know, all the surrounding areas knew that they killed the Jewish people. And he says, I would like to help you. He says, but what can I do for you? I say, would you allow us to sleep over there where you keep your cows and the horse in the barn-- what do you call it? A barn or whatever. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, let us-- we're not going to stay there  in the daytime. We'll stay in the woods. Maybe at night when it gets cold, let us in over there to sleep over. He was so nice, you know? And he says, yes, I will.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the man's name at all, first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I forgot his name. I don't remember his name. And we tried to-- we said, we have some things that we had hiding in our-- we made a false wail in the house we lived, and we hided there certain things. If you bring all the things, we'll give it to you away. We don't need it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think somebody came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, your daughter. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that would be good if she would go to the other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll stop her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, then I said-- then one day came-- there was already-- there was already Russian partisans walking around in that area, but we didn't know about that. So they came in, and they saw us walking in in the-- in the barn over there. And they recognized that we are--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. No, keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: They recognized--

speaker: interviewer
text: She sees him.

speaker: interviewee
text: --something that we are Jewish. So so she-- so he's-- so we run, and we hide ourselves over there. So they start talking to us behind the, the doors. And they says, came out, came out, he says. In Russian, they start talking. They says, [RUSSIAN]. And if not, we will kill you. We will-- we will take you. We will put a fire, and we'll burn the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You understood some Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they talked in Russian. They talked in Russian. I was suspicious. I thought, maybe they are Russian. But we didn't know. We thought that's the Ukrainian partisans. They were-- they were murderers. They were killing the Jewish people if they caught them. So we were afraid to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figured, I said to Benjamin, I said, you know what we're going to do? Let them put on fire the, the garage. If we'll be able to run way, we'll run away. If not, what can we do? Nothing. They'll kill us anyway. So in meantime, they went into the house. That was in the garage, and they went into the house. And then they see there was fresh bread was baking-- was baked up. The lady of the house baked up a lot of fresh bread. So they said-- they said, lady, we'll take the bread. So they put-- grabbed the bread and run away and let us in the-- they left us alone. They left us alone, so that's the way it should be, I guess. Close call.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked into the house afterwards. And we were sitting and talking with them. In meantime came a friend of ours. His name was Shmulek. He lived in a village not too far from there. And in fact is he lived later in Israel, and he died in Israel. And he asked the man-- he was a nice man-- he says, can I come and visit the boys, sometimes? I'm by myself, wandering around in the woods. He says, yeah, you may. One evening, he came in. We were not so happy because the end was not good. At that time, all you have to do by yourself, or if you were with somebody. If you were with company, I don't know what the end was. While he was there, he had a gun, that guy, Shmulek-- beautiful, nice-looking gun. And the guy, the Polack, asked him, show me your gun. And he starts showing him. We start monkeying around. And accidentally, the gun shot out and hit him in the leg, and he fell down. And the whole house filled with blood. He got run away, the guy, Shmulek, and left us.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife start crying. She says, what are you going to do? You almost burn my house, now my, my [INAUDIBLE]. And  she says, you have to leave. But please, she said, take me over. His brother lives but a kilometer away. It was up to here with snow. It was in the winter. It was a lot of snow. And they were just-- So we-- So we took it. We took her home and then came back, and we said, we're going to go away. So who do we go? Near, near, not too far, was a little-- a very deep wood, woods, the forest. So we went over there, and we were laying over there. While we were laying over there, her cow, I guess, ran away. And she was running after the cow. She was running after the cow.

speaker: interviewee
text: She comes in. She finds us over there. She just starts, god, please, she says, leave us alone. Go away from here because my husband is in the hospital now. And they're going to kill me too later. Get away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we say, OK, we'll go away. Go away. Where do we go? We move away from her a little bit further somewhere in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And to make the story short, that we-- we didn't show up for a while. You know, we didn't show up a w while. And then we decided, well, it got till early spring. It was already-- see, see, that was  in the winter when that happened. But then we were still laying around there, laying around there. And it was-- I don't know. Don't ask me how we survived. How we survived? No food and no clothes, freezing. We were so freezing, laying outside in the woods in 20 degree weather and sleeping at night. You can imagine what that is. I don't know. Maybe because we were young. I don't know. I don't have the answer for that. So when it got warm a little bit, we finally open our eyes. We figure, well, at least we had the sunshine, so we can go out. It was so tragic that we were so covered up, what do you call, lice. You know what that is? Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That we thought, if he-- if they didn't kill us, we're going to die from that. Because we, we cannot survive like that. So me and Benjamin cleaned it up ourselves a little bit while there was sun and we could get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we took a walk one time, and we met a Polish man walking. And he says, hey, boys, what are you doing? How did you survive? Everybody was killed. So we told him we survived. He says, you know what? Go to the woods. In that-- in that wood, the forest next to your village right where you lived, there is another family or two survived. And maybe you can get united with them. We took right away. We took-- what did we took? We didn't have anything [LAUGHS] to take anyway. We moved over there. And we came over there, and we find them. We find them. What was the name of that family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the family was the name Burak and Schuster. I have-- I had all their names here. I knew them. I knew them from before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schuster.

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew me. They knew me. We finally got in into that. And it was-- and there was-- we met a man who was walkin' over there. He recognized me. And he came in, and he fall on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were walkin' around in the-- in the forest for a long time, maybe  for hours until we find them. Why? Because the Polack told him, if you go in into the woods, in the forest, to find the people, he says, they have a shoemaker. He sees all this and fix the shoes over there. And he knocks with his hammer. If you'll hear the voice, you'll find you'll go after that voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the forest, there's also birds, which they eat wood. I mean, they eat wood. They knock on the wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Woodpecker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were following down the birds. [LAUGHS] And for hours, we couldn't find them. We didn't know where they are. But finally, this man was walkin' around, and he find us. He find us. And he says, OK. He called me by my first name. It's like he didn't know Benjamin. And he says, I'll bring you over. He brings us over to there. They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see it where they lived, and I said, how did you live here like this in a grave? We didn't live in a grave, in a grave level. We were always on the top, you know? And they were living in a grave. They were one family, a son and two [INAUDIBLE] two daughters. And there was another family with two sons. And there was about eight, 10 people. And they were so happy. They know me. And they says, well, you'll stay with us. You're not gonna to go back. I say, why will I go back? I'm not going to go back. So we stayed with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then also this-- came in one man, had a-- had a gun. One man had a gun. He says, I tell you what to do. Let's get organized. As long we have one gun, there is a place here that, that a Ukrainian lives, right next-- not too far from the grave with the 4,000 people. And he packed up his house full with clothes. He took it away from the-- from the people when they killed them. He says, let's go and take care of that-- of that guy. He says, we'll take sticks. Everybody's going to walk with a stick, and he'll think in the dark that it's rifles. But I have the gun, and we can take care of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were young boys, you know? So hunger, we were not that time already. Why? Because we used to go out on the fields and dig up potatoes, and carrots, and things like this. And we still got enough to eat. So we went over there at night, and we knocked in the door. And the guy says, who are you? He was home with his wife and the children. I says, we are partisans. Open. He opened the door. I says, we want something to eat. So he took out milk and sour cream, you know, and made some hot coffee, this, and then gave us to eat. After we finish eat, I say, what is all those things laying? Let, let us see what is it in your rooms. Why do you have it? I said, that's Jewish clothes. How come you packed up your house in Jewish clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we started talking with him. And, in the meantime, we took her with the children and we put them in a-- in a cupboard over there, so they wouldn't bother us, and, and we can have a talk with him. We can have a talk with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we couldn't get out from him too much. In the meantime, while we were talking with him, she, with the children, ran out somewhere to a window over there, and they went away. They went away. When we see she's not there, we say, well, we have to run away because she might go and tell some, you know, neighbors or whoever it is. And they'll come here with and they'll kill us. So we took him, the guy, and we tied him up with a rope. We put him on the wagon. We took his wagon, and we took his big-- what do you call a [HEBREW]? Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one we make ham. A pig.

speaker: interviewer
text: A pig. Oh, OK. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: He had-- he had a big pig over there. So we took that pig, and we put him up on the-- on the-- on the wagon, on his wagon, because we figured the pig will give us for weeks to eat something, you know? [LAUGHS] And we took the pig, and we took him to the woods. And we told him, this is what you're going to get for what you did to the Jewish people over there. And we left him over there. And we took the pig away, and we left the horse with the wagon. We just let him go, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and then, then we came back in the woods. And then we lived over there in the woods for a long time. We lived over there from the summer-- from the summer of 1943 till, till spring 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, no, no, I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, we, we didn't-- we didn't live-- we, we were in the woods till, more or less, the middle of the winter, a few months, yeah, together. But then we met the Russian partisans-- that's what it was-- in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the summer, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: of '43, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the leader of that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fyodorov was the leader of the whole entire partisan group, which were in the tens of thousands. When they came into us that time in the woods, there wasn't too many of them, was on horses, just a few officers dressed, you know, like Russians. And they caught us walking around in the fields over there. And they said, stop. If not, I'm going to shoot. I'm going to shoot. So we stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they says, who are you? We told them we are Jewish people. We survived, and we are living here in the woods. He says to us-- he was a very nice man, an officer-- he says, don't you worry. You are in safe hand now.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll come-- the-- our-- the head of our group is in Moscow. He's going to come in with a plane in the next few days. Just show me your place where you are, and we will come with wagons. And we will take you away. We'll keep you in the partisans, and you don't have to worry from now on. You're safe. And that's the way it was. In a few days, he came and he took us-- every one of us, the children, the few there was, and the women, and us. They took us into the partisans. And then we were in the partisans from-- not from-- the winter of 1943 till the spring of 1944. And the partisans, I have to tell you, we did a lot of damage and destroyed a lot of the Germans. We used to go and put up on the rails ammunition, bombs which blew up the rails. And they used to collapse over there where they used to go and travel soldiers to the front, or ammunition, or clothes, or food, whatever-- we used to destroy this on the way. We used to do to them so much damage-- unbelievable, the, the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the name of that area again where you were in operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, what it called Volhynia Gubernia. Ukrainian. It was Ukrainian, Volhynia. Volhynia was like, here, a state. Not a big state like California, but a small state which has only 5,000 people, or 6,000 people there [INAUDIBLE]. That's Volhynia Gubernia, and that's what we were over there with the-- with the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then who was supplying all the materials and food and weapons and everything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, we had plenty. There was no shortage of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it coming from Russia or locally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we used to go in a village and just clean them up. [LAUGHS] They didn't play with them, Russians. And the-- and the fight, of course, was with the Ukrainian. Not so much with, with the Polish people like with the Ukrainian. Was constantly fights and fights and fights. And then to make it a little bit the, the story shorter, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] only one thing we felt good, at least we felt like we are something, human beings. We can take revenge. We can fight, not just go to the grave and be shot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- in the spring of 1944, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were already, at that time-- I in the, the other people from the-- when we got out from the-- we were liberated by the-- by the Russian Army, you know? So we were free. I mean, you could go where we wanted to go. But where do you go? Who can we go home? We don't have no home. We don't have nothing. The war was still going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the war with Stalin was full. Because that was in a spring of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: They started already to collapse, the, the Germans because the Russians came and liberated us, that territory. So it mean the whole territory, all the way from us to Moscow-- I don't know, maybe 2,000 miles, I don't know many, how far-- it was already liberated. Not all of Poland was liberated, but practically all from Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were settled in a station which was not too far between our place and Lutsk. It was from Lutsk only [? about ?] maybe 15, 20 kilometer. There was a railroad station, a big railroad station. So we got together in one house, and we were there. When we came in into that house, I got sick. I got sick on typhus. That was a very popular sickness during the wartime. And they took me into the hospital over there, the Russians. Took me in the Russian, took me into the hospital. Took me into the hospital, and it's a-- how? It is interesting that the man that was taking care of me was a Jewish doctor. He survived the war. He was there, and I met him later in Israel 30 years ago. He was taking care of me in that hospital in 1943 . Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember his name offhand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's good to know as many names as we can get.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's so hard to remember names because we didn't write it down anything, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, when I got out from the hospital, I was already weak, of course, white like a-- like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ghost.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] a dead person. And I was in that house. There were in that house about 10, 15 people, Jewish people. And I was laying on the sofa. Suddenly, the door opens and, and a Russian officer walks in. He was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't speak very well Jewish, but he still [INAUDIBLE]. And he-- and he says-- he shows with his finger, he says, who's that person? Why do you keep that person here in the home? He thought, for me, that I'm dead. I was laying over there. That-- that's the way I looked. So I opened my mouth, and I said to him in Russian-- because I could speak Russia. I was working with  the Russians. I was in the partisan. I was two years in the restaurant with the Russians. I knew Russia very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I'm not dead yet. I'm still alive. He opened his eyes. And he says, tell me something about you. Well, I told him the story how I survived, and this and that and that. He says to me, I'll tell you what, I have a big job for you. I'm going on vacation tomorrow. And I'll be away for four weeks because I didn't have vacation since the war started in '39. If I will find you here, and you will be all right, you will be alive, he says, I'll take you with me, and I'll give you one of the best jobs-- just like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I figured, well, he says, it's a war. Who knows? [LAUGHS]. The war. We heard the, the fights was going on. He left, and in a month later, he walked into the house. He came to the time. It was just like from heaven. The front-- the fights did not move from that city, which was not too far from us. It was standing in one place. They didn't move, the Russian Army. And he came in. He walked in. He says, oh, he says, you look good now. He says, take your things and come with me. I says, I don't have anything to take. [LAUGHS] I say that's the [INAUDIBLE] thing, eh? Torn pants, torn shirt. [LAUGHS] That's all I had on myself, nothing else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he takes me in his machine and taked me away. And they occupied the palace from the Polish people, you know. And he takes me in, and there was another Russian guy. He was managing-- he was managing the warehouse which we call here in America, PX, if you ever heard what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Drugstore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which supplies all the food for the army and things like this. And over there, they called this [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the name. And he says to the guy, you giving me [INAUDIBLE] everything what is here in the warehouse. From now on, he'll be the manager here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came in over there. There came in right away a guy, another officer, who also was a Jewish man. There was a lot of Jewish people over there. Do you remember any of the names as we go along here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: None of them? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only name I knew from that guy that came in, the officer. His name was Fima.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell  it? Well, we'll get it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, his name, I remember. The other guys, [? I ?] cannot remember. And another officer comes in, and he says, oh, you've got such a big job. And we had not only food-- all the drinks in the world, all the, the vodkas, all the champagnes. Because we used to walk in in a city and take away everything from the-- from the Polish warehouses. And caviar, everything. I'm telling you. I started drinking and eating like, like I forgot that I lived through the war, [LAUGHS] you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: One thing along the way, you said when we talked previously that as you were passing through Lublin and some of the other towns, you still didn't know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'll come to that in a second.

words.text: I'll come to that in a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's next? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll come to that in a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting ahead of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm so close to that. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- it's episodes that I like to tell 'em. That the-- that the guy-- I said to the guy, well, I don't have any-- I'm ashamed to stay here. You come in all dressed with the-- with the medals and with the beautiful uniforms. And I'm-- I had torn pants. He says-- and I'm so-- I say, here you come--

speaker: interviewee
text: He knocks me over my neck and says, oh, don't be a fool. He says, you're going to be dressed in another few days just like a general. They will bring you clothes. They will bring you whatever you want. Whatever your heart desires, they will bring it to you. [LAUGHS] Because you sell everything here, what they need, you know? The truth was, it was like that. I became friendly with all the officers and everything, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we start going-- you know, beating the Germans and go with the front. And I was all of a sudden in the rear with my-- all staff and what I had in supply in big automobiles. We passed by Lublin that time too. But I didn't have no idea. I didn't know anything that there is concentration camps-- there was concentration camps and people were gassed, and things like at Majdanek, like at Auschwitz, like nothing. We never talked about it, and they didn't tell me. Maybe they knew something. Maybe the general knew. I was very friendly with the general too. But he never said a word, and I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even late '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing I knew till, till the war was over. That was in '44. We stopped, finally, at the place which was Minsk Mazowiecki, the name. That's where my wife was there. It was only about, I don't know, I think about 20 kilometers from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fights in the war was on. They were fighting till the last man, the Germans. They were already in Warsaw. You can imagine, from Moscow to Warsaw? How far they had to go and give up, all the territories? Poland was almost claimed already. But they still were fighting with the airplanes, day and night, and the tanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the officer, this one, and, and there was another Jewish major too, we all became so friendly. He says to me, hey, you Polack. He called  me Polack because he knew I was born in Poland. And I know Polish too, to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says to me, hey, you Polack, let's take the jeep and let's go to the city over there, and ride not too far, a few kilometer. Let's find if there's any Jewish survived. It was interesting for them to know. I say, OK, let's go. So we sit down, all three of us, in a jeep and the chauffeur. And we stopped on the road, and I see a Polack. And I ask him in Polish, can you tell me, is there any Jewish people here in this village, in this little town, or whatever?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, yes, I heard, he says, for-- since a few weeks, since maybe two weeks, we liberated that area. We liberated that territory. And we liberated those Jewish people too. They got liberated because of the Russian Army. And I said, sit down here on the jeep, and come with us and show us who are the houses. He came. He show us. We let  him go. I walked into the house. Five boys, four girls-- I could cry to look at them. They were bones, not-- hungry. They didn't eat, without shoes, torn clothes. And I just-- [SIGHS] I walked over. The, the officer, the major, walked over to them and started talking to them. They were afraid, the girls, to talk with him. One of the girls was my wife.
